Fishes as our food

index of concepts **Fishes as our food**updated May 08

The fish we eat are either captured or cultured in fish farms.

Fish is also used to produce fish meal which is fed other livestock that we eventually eat, mostly poultry and farmed fish and shrimps.

From the 2006 UN FAO Study of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

Nearly half the fish consumed as food worldwide are raised on fish farms rather than caught in the wild. While in 1980 just 9 percent of the fish consumed by human beings came from aquaculture, today 43 percent does, the report shows.

Globally, consumer demand for fish continues to climb, especially in affluent, developed nations which accounted for 81% of all fish imports in value in 2004.

But levels of captures of fish in the wild have remained roughly stable since the mid-1980s. There is little chance of any significant increases in catches beyond these levels.

Global assessment of wild marine fish stocks found that out of the nearly 600 species monitored, 52 percent are fully exploited while 25 percent are either overexploited (17%), depleted (7%) or recovering from depletion (1%). Twenty percent are moderately exploited, with just three percent ranked as underexploited.

Levelling off in wild catches, coupled with a growing world population and increasing per capita demand for fish, spells trouble. FAO’s report estimates that an additional 40 million tonnes of aquatic food will be required by 2030 – just to maintain current levels of consumption.

The only option for meeting future demand for fish is by farming them. Freshwater culture continued to dominate, followed by mariculture and brackish-water culture. Carps accounted for 40 percent of all production of fish, crustaceans and molluscs. The period 2000-04 saw strong growth in production of crustaceans, in particular, and of marine fish.

Shrimp continues to be the most important commodity traded in value terms, accounting for 16.5 percent of the total value of internationally traded fishery products in 2004, followed by groundfish (10.2 percent), tuna (8.7 percent) and salmon (8.5 percent).

More than 2.6 billion people get at least 20% of their animal proten from fish.

Jobs in fishing and aquaculture has grown faster than the world�s population, and faster than employment in traditional agriculture. In 2004, an estimated 41 million people worked as fishers and fish farmers, the great majority of these in developing countries, principally in Asia.

  • Nearly half of all fish eaten today farmed, not caught Aquaculture only way to meet surging demand, but challenges to future growth loom FAO website 4 Sep 06
  • State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture 2006 on the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) website.
